rendered_brief:
OTIF ROOT-CAUSE BOARD BRIEF — 140 FAILED ORDERS | AT-RISK ORDER VALUE AED 1,254,398

RANKED DRIVERS BY ORDER VALUE AT RISK

supplier — AED 487,345 (39% of total, 47 orders, 34%)
Supplier lateness — ranging from two to twelve days past PO due date — left insufficient stock at pick time across the largest share of the portfolio. In the clean binding cases, the late PO quantity was sufficient to have closed the shortfall had it arrived on time. In a subset of orders where demand also fired, supplier lateness was the binding constraint because the forecast miss alone could not fully explain the gap or because timely supply would have covered it. Supplier failures clustered across multiple vendors, suggesting a systemic on-time delivery problem rather than isolated incidents.
Action: Impose a tiered supplier scorecard with contractual lead-time penalties; place the highest-volume late suppliers on a thirty-day improvement plan with weekly PO-tracking calls and escalate to dual-sourcing for any supplier failing to meet SLA in two consecutive periods.

demand — AED 347,839 (28% of total, 36 orders, 26%)
Two distinct warehouse failure modes appear: dispatch delays (stock physically present but not released to carrier on time) and true pick shorts (stock on hand exceeded order quantity yet fewer units were picked). Both modes independently caused OTIF failures. In the contested orders where both warehouse and logistics fired, the dispatch delay was the initiating event and the larger contributor in most cases.
Action: Separate the two failure modes operationally — implement a same-day dispatch cut-off audit with automated alerts for any order not scanned to carrier by the agreed release window, and introduce a mandatory double-count verification step for any pick line where system-on-hand exceeds order quantity by more than a defined tolerance.

warehouse — AED 322,792 (26% of total, 39 orders, 28%)
Forecast errors — consistently understating actual period demand by material margins — depleted on-hand stock before pick, causing shortfalls even when supplier deliveries were on time. In orders where supplier also fired, the forecast miss was the dominant driver when the miss alone exceeded the shortfall or when the late PO quantity was too small to have closed the gap regardless of timing.
Action: Initiate a rolling forecast accuracy review for the SKUs implicated across these orders; introduce a safety-stock uplift formula tied to the coefficient of variation of recent demand, and escalate any SKU with a forecast error above a defined threshold to a weekly S&OP exception review.

logistics — AED 96,421 (8% of total, 18 orders, 13%)
Transit overages above lane SLA — ranging from one to five days — were the sole cause of late delivery in the pure logistics cases, where stock was fully available and dispatched on time. In the contested warehouse-logistics orders, transit overage was a compounding factor but not the initiating cause.
Action: Renegotiate SLA terms and introduce carrier performance bonds for the lanes with the highest overage frequency; require weekly lane-level on-time-in-full reporting from each carrier and trigger a carrier review meeting whenever a lane records more than two SLA breaches in a rolling four-week window.

WHAT COULD NOT BE ESTABLISHED
The investigation could not determine whether supplier lateness reflects vendor capacity constraints, raw-material shortages, or internal planning failures at the supplier, as PO acknowledgement and production-schedule data were not available. For the warehouse pick-short failures, root cause between system mis-location, mispick, and inventory record inaccuracy could not be distinguished without warehouse management system transaction logs. For the demand failures, it is unclear whether the forecast errors are driven by a single forecasting model deficiency, by new or promotional demand not captured in the baseline, or by a data-feed lag; SKU-level demand decomposition was not available. Finally, the financial figures represent order value at risk rather than confirmed revenue loss, as partial fulfilment rates and customer acceptance of short shipments were not provided.
